input.content:
You are a member of an Advisory Council. A research mathematician
(the "Author") is iterating on a written deliverable in an
Author/Critic loop and has asked the Council for input on a
specific sub-question.

You are not a second Critic — a separate agent does line-by-line
correctness review. Your role is closer to a research collaborator:
when the Author has hit a wall on a sub-problem or wants a
different angle, you suggest alternative approaches, point at
adjacent literature you happen to know about, propose
decompositions, or share intuition about which directions are
likely to be fruitful.

Even when the Author phrases the question as "is X correct?",
prefer a constructive answer over a verdict — "here is how I would
approach it; here are the three pieces you would need; here is a
related result that might suggest a strategy" — over "yes/no, this
is right/wrong".

Read the current `answer.tex`, `research_notes.tex`, and
`references.bib` for context, then engage with the Author's
question.

Be opinionated where you have a real angle to offer. If you are
not sure, say "uncertain" rather than confabulating. Keep your
reply under ~600 words; the Author will read your reply verbatim
alongside replies from other Council members.

### Author's question for the Council ###
For the Dirichlet skew stochastic heat equation with drift \(\delta_0(u)\), can one prove all-starting fixed-time or resolvent absolute continuity \(P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\pi\) or \(R_\lambda(x,\cdot)\ll\pi\) by a “late-noise splitting” argument?

More concretely, for \(t>\varepsilon>0\),
\[
u_t=S_\varepsilon u_{t-\varepsilon}+Z_\varepsilon
+\int_{t-\varepsilon}^t S_{t-s}\delta_0(u_s)\,ds,
\]
where \(Z_\varepsilon\) has covariance equivalent to Brownian bridge measure. The drift convolution should be smoother in high modes than \(Z_\varepsilon\), but it depends on the same recent noise. A mere random Cameron–Martin shift is not enough, since dependent shifts can collapse Gaussian measure onto a null hyperplane. Is there a rigorous adaptedness/Malliavin/triangular-map argument here, or a known theorem for fixed-time absolute continuity of SPDEs with distributional drift that could apply? Please suggest a concrete proof architecture or explain the sharp obstruction.

### Current answer.tex ###
```tex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb R}
\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb E}
\newcommand{\1}{\mathbf 1}
\newcommand{\cE}{\mathcal E}
\newcommand{\Var}{\operatorname{Var}}
\newcommand{\capacity}{\operatorname{Cap}}
\newcommand{\TV}{\operatorname{TV}}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf Invariant measures for the Dirichlet skew stochastic heat equation}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Let \(E=C_0([0,1])\) and let \(S_t\) be the Dirichlet heat semigroup.
I use the ABLM regularized/mild interpretation specified in the problem,
including the assumed Dirichlet-boundary extension.  I also use the following
pointwise interpretation of the stated weak convergence of regularizations:
for every bounded continuous \(\Phi:E\to\R\), deterministic \(x\in E\), and
\(t>0\),
\[
        P_t^n\Phi(x)\longrightarrow P_t\Phi(x).        \tag{A}
\]
The drift is formally a gradient drift:
\[
        \delta_0=-\frac12\frac{d}{dr}(-2H_+),\qquad
        H_+(r)=\1_{\{r>0\}} .
\]
If \(\gamma\) denotes Brownian bridge measure on \(E\), the corresponding
Gibbs probability is
\[
   \Lambda(v)=\int_0^1\1_{\{v(x)>0\}}\,dx,\qquad
   \pi(dv)=Z^{-1}e^{2\Lambda(v)}\gamma(dv).       \tag{1}
\]
The density is bounded above and below because \(0\le\Lambda\le1\), hence
\(\pi\sim\gamma\), and \(\gamma\) has full support on \(E\).  The convention
at zero is immaterial since a Brownian bridge spends zero Lebesgue time at
zero:
\[
   \E_\gamma\int_0^1\1_{\{\beta(x)=0\}}\,dx
    =\int_0^1\mathbb P(\beta(x)=0)\,dx=0 .
\]

\section*{What the approximation proves}

Set
\[
   B_n(r)=\int_{-\infty}^rG_{1/n}(s)\,ds,\qquad
   \pi_n(dv)=Z_n^{-1}\exp\!\left(2\int_0^1B_n(v(x))\,dx\right)\gamma(dv).
\]
Then \(0\le B_n\le1\), \(B_n(r)\to H_+(r)\) for \(r\ne0\), and dominated
convergence gives
\[
       \|\pi_n-\pi\|_{\TV}\longrightarrow0 .          \tag{2}
\]
For fixed \(n\), \(v\mapsto G_{1/n}(v)\) is a bounded Lipschitz
\(L^2(0,1)\)-valued Nemytskii map.  The usual Galerkin/Dirichlet-form
identification for semilinear stochastic heat equations gives the
reversible form
\[
   \cE_n(F,G)=\frac12\int_E\langle DF,DG\rangle_{L^2(0,1)}\,d\pi_n
\]
on smooth cylinder functions and its closure in \(L^2(\pi_n)\).  Equivalently,
for cylinder \(F,G\),
\[
 \int G\,L_nF\,d\pi_n
   =-\frac12\int\langle DF,DG\rangle_{L^2(0,1)}\,d\pi_n
   =\int F\,L_nG\,d\pi_n,                         \tag{3}
\]
where
\[
 L_nF(v)=\frac12{\rm Tr}\,D^2F(v)
       +\left\langle \frac12\Delta_Dv+G_{1/n}(v),DF(v)\right\rangle .
\]
Thus \(P_t^n\) is reversible and invariant with respect to \(\pi_n\).

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:limit}
Under (A), \(\pi\) is invariant for \(P_t\).  Moreover \(P_t\) has a
symmetric Markov contraction extension to \(L^2(\pi)\), and there is
\(c>0\) such that
\[
     \Var_{\pi}(P_t f)\le e^{-2ct}\Var_\pi(f),
     \qquad f\in L^2(\pi),\ t\ge0.                 \tag{4}
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
For \(f,g\in C_b(E)\), (A), dominated convergence, (2), and invariance of
\(\pi_n\) yield
\[
 \int P_tf\,d\pi
 =\lim_n\int P_t^nf\,d\pi
  =\lim_n\int P_t^nf\,d\pi_n
  =\lim_n\int f\,d\pi_n
  =\int f\,d\pi .
\]
The same argument with (3) gives
\[
 \int g\,P_tf\,d\pi=\int f\,P_tg\,d\pi,\qquad f,g\in C_b(E).
\]
Since \(E\) is Polish, equality against bounded continuous functions
determines the relevant finite measures; invariance also gives the Jensen
bound \(\|P_t f\|_{L^2(\pi)}\le\|f\|_{L^2(\pi)}\).  Hence \(P_t\) extends
uniquely from \(C_b(E)\) to a symmetric Markov contraction on \(L^2(\pi)\).

Brownian bridge measure satisfies a Gaussian Poincare inequality, and the
densities \(d\pi_n/d\gamma\) are bounded above and below uniformly in \(n\).
Consequently,
\[
     \Var_{\pi_n}(F)\le C\,\cE_n(F,F)                 \tag{5}
\]
with \(C\) independent of \(n\).  By the spectral theorem for the reversible
semigroups \(P_t^n\),
\[
     \Var_{\pi_n}(P_t^n f)\le e^{-2ct}\Var_{\pi_n}(f) \tag{6}
\]
for bounded continuous \(f\), with \(c=C^{-1}\) after changing constants.
Letting \(n\to\infty\) in (6), using (A) and (2), gives (4) for bounded
continuous \(f\); density of \(C_b(E)\) in \(L^2(\pi)\) gives the general
case.
\end{proof}

The spectral gap already settles all invariant probabilities which are
absolutely continuous with respect to \(\pi\).

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:ac}
If \(\eta\) is \(P_t\)-invariant and \(\eta\ll\pi\), then \(\eta=\pi\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Write \(\eta=h\pi\), \(h\ge0\), \(\int h\,d\pi=1\).  For bounded
\(\varphi\),
\[
 \int\varphi h\,d\pi=\int P_t\varphi\,h\,d\pi
     =\int\varphi\,P_t h\,d\pi ,
\]
where the last identity follows first for bounded \(h\) by symmetry and
then for \(h\in L^1(\pi)\) by truncation and the \(L^1\)-contraction of the
Markov operator.  Thus \(P_t h=h\) in \(L^1(\pi)\).  For \(a>0\), put
\(h_a=h\wedge a\).  Since \(r\mapsto r\wedge a\) is concave,
\[
       P_t h_a\le (P_t h)\wedge a=h_a .
\]
The two sides have the same \(\pi\)-integral, hence \(P_t h_a=h_a\).
Now \(h_a\in L^2(\pi)\), so (4) implies \(\Var_\pi(h_a)=0\).  Thus each
\(h_a\) is constant, and letting \(a\uparrow\infty\) gives \(h\equiv1\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Exact criteria which would imply full uniqueness}

The unresolved point is whether every invariant probability is absolutely
continuous with respect to \(\pi\).  The following criteria isolate the
needed all-starting-points input.

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:resolvent}
If, for some \(\lambda>0\),
\[
     R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll \pi,\qquad
     R_\lambda^P(x,A):=\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}P_t(x,A)\,dt,
     \quad x\in E,                                  \tag{7}
\]
then \(\pi\) is the only invariant probability measure.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\eta\) be invariant.  If \(\pi(A)=0\), then
\[
   \eta(A)=\lambda\int_E R_\lambda^P(x,A)\,\eta(dx)=0.
\]
Hence \(\eta\ll\pi\), and Lemma \ref{lem:ac} applies.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:sf}
Either of the following implies (7): \(P_t\) is strong Feller for every
\(t>0\), or, for one \(\lambda>0\), \(R_\lambda^P\) maps bounded Borel
functions to continuous functions.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
If \(\pi(A)=0\), invariance gives \(\int P_t1_A\,d\pi=0\), respectively
\(\int R_\lambda^P1_A\,d\pi=0\).  The relevant function is nonnegative and
continuous.  Since \(\pi\) has full support, it must vanish identically.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[eventual equicontinuity criterion]\label{lem:eprop}
Assume that for every bounded Lipschitz \(f:E\to\R\), every \(x\in E\), and
every \(\varepsilon>0\), there are a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(x\) and
\(t_0>0\) such that
\[
        |P_t f(y)-P_t f(x)|\le\varepsilon,
        \qquad y\in U,\ t\ge t_0 .                  \tag{8}
\]
Then \(\pi\) is the unique invariant probability measure.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
By (4), \(P_t f\to\pi(f)\) in \(L^2(\pi)\).  Fix \(x\) and choose \(U\) as
in (8).  Since \(\pi\) has full support, \(\pi(U)>0\), and for \(t\ge t_0\),
\[
 |P_t f(x)-\pi(f)|
 \le \varepsilon+
 \left(\pi(U)^{-1}\int_U |P_t f(y)-\pi(f)|^2\,\pi(dy)\right)^{1/2}.
\]
Letting \(t\to\infty\), then \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), gives
\(P_t f(x)\to\pi(f)\) for every \(x\).  Invariance of any \(\eta\) and
dominated convergence give \(\eta(f)=\pi(f)\).  Bounded Lipschitz functions
determine probability measures on \(E\).
\end{proof}

\section*{The Bounebache--Zambotti exceptional set}

For the same bounded potential, Bounebache--Zambotti construct the
Dirichlet form
\[
        \cE(F,G)=\frac12\int_E\langle DF,DG\rangle_{L^2(0,1)}\,d\pi .
\]
Their absolute-continuity theorem gives a resolvent density only outside a
properly exceptional set \(N\) (the displayed kernel is extended arbitrarily
on \(N\)).  Thus it proves \(R_\lambda(y,\cdot)\ll\pi\) for quasi-every
starting point \(y\), not for every deterministic \(y\in E\).  If the ABLM
and BZ solutions are identified, uniqueness follows as soon as invariant
laws are known not to charge \(N\).

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:entrance}
Let \(N\) be a BZ exceptional set such that \(R_\lambda^P(y,\cdot)\ll\pi\)
for \(y\notin N\).  If
\[
          P_s(x,N)=0,\qquad x\in E,\ s>0,              \tag{9}
\]
then (7) holds for every \(x\in E\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\pi(A)=0\).  For \(y\notin N\), \(R_\lambda^P(y,A)=0\).  For
\(\varepsilon>0\), the resolvent identity gives
\[
 R_\lambda^P(x,A)
 \le \varepsilon
  +e^{-\lambda\varepsilon}\int_E R_\lambda^P(y,A)P_\varepsilon(x,dy)
 \le \varepsilon+\lambda^{-1}P_\varepsilon(x,N).
\]
Use (9) and let \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\).
\end{proof}

A non-pointwise way to get the same conclusion is a capacity-Krylov
estimate.

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:krylov}
Suppose \(R_\lambda^P(y,\cdot)\ll\pi\) for \(y\notin N\), where \(N\) is a
BZ exceptional set.  Assume also that for every compact \(K\subset E\) and
\(0<\varepsilon<T\), there are \(C,\alpha>0\) such that for every open
\(O\subset E\),
\[
 \sup_{x\in K}\E_x\int_\varepsilon^T\1_O(u_s)\,ds
      \le C\,\capacity(O)^\alpha .                       \tag{10}
\]
Then every invariant probability charges no BZ exceptional set.  Hence
\(\eta\ll\pi\) for every invariant \(\eta\), and Lemma \ref{lem:ac} gives
uniqueness.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\eta\) be invariant and \(O\) open.  By tightness choose compact
\(K\) with \(\eta(K^c)<\delta\).  Stationarity and (10) imply
\[
 (T-\varepsilon)\eta(O)
 =\int_E\E_x\int_\varepsilon^T\1_O(u_s)\,ds\,\eta(dx)
 \le C\capacity(O)^\alpha+(T-\varepsilon)\delta .
\]
If \(N_0\) has zero capacity, outer regularity gives open \(O\supset N_0\)
with \(C\capacity(O)^\alpha<(T-\varepsilon)\delta\).  Hence
\(\eta(N_0)\le2\delta\), and then \(\delta\downarrow0\) gives
\(\eta(N_0)=0\).  In particular \(\eta(N)=0\).  If \(\pi(A)=0\), invariance
and the quasi-everywhere resolvent absolute continuity on \(N^c\) give
\[
   \eta(A)=\lambda\int_{N^c}R_\lambda^P(y,A)\,\eta(dy)=0 .
\]
Thus \(\eta\ll\pi\), and Lemma \ref{lem:ac} applies.
\end{proof}

\section*{Remaining open issues}

The preceding argument proves uniqueness of all invariant laws once one
can rule out invariant mass on the BZ exceptional set.  What remains
unproved from the hypotheses stated in the problem is one of the following
all-starting-points upgrades:
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{(i)}& \text{resolvent absolute continuity (7),}\\
\text{(ii)}& \text{strong/resolvent Feller regularity,}\\
\text{(iii)}& \text{eventual equicontinuity (8),}\\
\text{(iv)}& \text{entrance into }N^c\text{ as in (9),}\\
\text{(v)}& \text{the capacity-Krylov estimate (10).}
\end{array}
\]
The regularized Gibbs measures and semigroups give the invariant reversible
law \(\pi\) and the \(L^2(\pi)\) spectral gap, but these are insensitive to
changes of a Markov process on a \(\pi\)-null exceptional set.  Therefore the
present proof establishes uniqueness among invariant laws absolutely
continuous with respect to \(\pi\) and gives precise sufficient criteria for
full uniqueness, but it does not yet prove or disprove the original
all-starting-points statement without an additional estimate excluding
exceptional invariant measures.

\begin{thebibliography}{9}
\bibitem{ABLM}
S. Athreya, O. Butkovsky, K. L\^e and L. Mytnik,
\emph{Well-posedness of stochastic heat equation with distributional drift
and skew stochastic heat equation}, Comm. Pure Appl. Math. 77 (2024),
2708--2777.

\bibitem{ABLMweakmild}
S. Athreya, O. Butkovsky, K. L\^e and L. Mytnik,
\emph{Analytically weak and mild solutions to stochastic heat equation with
irregular drift}, Stoch. PDE: Anal. Comp. (2025).

\bibitem{BZ}
S. K. Bounebache and L. Zambotti,
\emph{A skew stochastic heat equation}, J. Theoret. Probab. 27 (2014),
168--201.

\bibitem{FOT}
M. Fukushima, Y. Oshima and M. Takeda,
\emph{Dirichlet Forms and Symmetric Markov Processes}, de Gruyter, 2011.
\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}

```

### Current research_notes.tex ###
```tex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
\begin{document}
\section*{Research notes}

Problem: uniqueness of invariant probability for Dirichlet stochastic heat
equation on $[0,1]$ with additive space-time white noise and distributional
drift $\delta_0(u)$, under the ABLM regularized/mild interpretation and
regularisation by $G_{1/n}$.

Key interpretation:
\[
\delta_0(r)=-\frac12(-2{\bf1}_{r>0})',
\]
so the equation is a singular gradient SPDE with bounded potential
$f(r)=-2H_+(r)$.  For smooth approximation $B_n'=G_{1/n}$ with
$B_n(r)=\int_{-\infty}^rG_{1/n}(s)\,ds$, the invariant measure of the
regularized gradient equation is
\[
\pi_n(dv)=Z_n^{-1}\exp\left(2\int_0^1 B_n(v(x))\,dx\right)\gamma(dv),
\]
where $\gamma$ is Brownian bridge law (the invariant measure of the
Dirichlet OU/string process).  Since $0\le B_n\le1$ and $B_n\to H_+$
away from zero, while a Brownian bridge spends zero Lebesgue time at
zero,
\[
\pi_n\to
\pi(dv)=Z^{-1}\exp\left(2\int_0^1{\bf1}_{v(x)>0}\,dx\right)\gamma(dv)
\]
in total variation.  Densities $d\pi_n/d\gamma$ and $d\pi/d\gamma$ are
bounded above and below by deterministic positive constants.

Round-1 robust result obtained: assuming pointwise weak convergence of
regularized semigroups on bounded continuous observables,
\[
P_t^n\Phi(x)\to P_t\Phi(x),\qquad x\in C_0,\ t>0,
\]
one can pass reversibility/invariance and a uniform spectral gap from
$P^n$ to the actual ABLM semigroup $P$ without invoking
Bounebache--Zambotti:
\[
\int gP_tf\,d\pi=\int fP_tg\,d\pi,\qquad
\operatorname{Var}_\pi(P_tf)\le e^{-2ct}\operatorname{Var}_\pi(f).
\]
Key estimates:
\[
\operatorname{Var}_{\pi_n}(F)\le C\mathcal E_n(F,F),\qquad
\mathcal E_n(F,F)=\frac12\int\|DF\|_{L^2}^2\,d\pi_n,
\]
with $C$ uniform in $n$, by Gaussian Poincare for $\gamma$ plus bounded
density perturbation.  Then pass the $L^2$ contraction/spectral gap to
the limit using total variation convergence $\pi_n\to\pi$ and bounded
pointwise convergence $P_t^nf\to P_tf$.

Abstract uniqueness mechanism:
If $\eta$ is invariant and the actual resolvent satisfies
\[
R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\pi\quad\hbox{for every }x,
\]
then $\eta=\lambda\eta R_\lambda^P\ll\pi$.  With $h=d\eta/d\pi$,
symmetry gives $P_th=h$ in $L^1(\pi)$.  For $h_a=h\wedge a$, concavity
gives $h_a\ge P_th_a$ and equal integrals force equality.  Since
$h_a\in L^2$, the spectral gap forces $h_a$ constant.  Varying $a$
implies $h$ constant, hence $\eta=\pi$.

Central unresolved issue:
Need pointwise resolvent absolute continuity for the actual ABLM
semigroup, not only for the $L^2(\pi)$ Dirichlet-form version and not
only quasi-everywhere.  BZ Proposition 2.5 gives an $L^2$ resolvent kernel
$\rho_\lambda(x,dy)\ll\nu(dy)$ for all $x$ (setting it to zero on an
exceptional set) and transition absolute continuity for all
non-exceptional initial conditions.  BZ Proposition 3.1 constructs a
weak local-time solution for quasi-every initial condition.  ABLM
uniqueness plus the 2025 weak/mild equivalence paper plausibly identify
the BZ process with the ABLM mild process for quasi-every initial point,
but this still does not prove that arbitrary invariant measures cannot
charge the exceptional set, nor that $R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\pi$ for
$x$ in that set.

Potential routes to close the gap:
1. Uniform heat-kernel/capacity estimates for $P_t^n$: prove
   $P_t^n(x,A)\le C_{t,x}\,\mathrm{Cap}_n(A)^\alpha$ or
   $\|dP_t^n(x,\cdot)/d\pi_n\|_{L^2(\pi_n)}\le C_{t,x}$ with constants
   stable as $n\to\infty$ (perhaps only for $t$ large).  This would pass
   AC to the limit and imply uniqueness via convergence to equilibrium.
2. Strict version of BZ process: prove that the ABLM process is the
   strict all-starting-points Hunt process associated with the BZ
   Dirichlet form, and that BZ's resolvent kernel represents its
   resolvent at every deterministic $x$.
3. Entrance from exceptional sets: let $N$ be the BZ exceptional set.
   Show $P_s^P(x,N)=0$ for every $x$ and $s>0$.  Then q.e. resolvent AC
   plus the Markov property gives all-$x$ resolvent AC:
   $R_\lambda(x,A)\le\int_0^s e^{-\lambda t}P_t(x,A)dt$ for
   $\pi(A)=0$, then let $s\downarrow0$.
4. Prove uniqueness of invariant measures without pointwise AC by showing
   every invariant stationary solution has enough finite-energy/Sobolev
   regularity not to charge zero-capacity sets.  Stationarity and the
   nondegenerate additive noise imply one-dimensional projections have no
   atoms by the occupation formula, but this is far weaker than avoiding
   all Gaussian-capacity-zero sets.

Useful literature facts:
- BZ use $\nu(dx)\propto \exp(-\int f(x_r)dr)\mu(dx)$.  For
  $f=-2H_+$ this is exactly $\pi$.
- BZ equation has singular term
  $-\frac12\int f(da)\partial_\theta\ell^a_{t,\theta}$; for
  $f=-2H_+$ this is $\partial_\theta\ell^0$, formally
  $\delta_0(u(t,\theta))$.
- ABLM 2024 proves strong existence/uniqueness for $b=\kappa\delta_0$
  and convergence of smooth approximations; it explicitly did not treat
  Dirichlet boundary conditions, but the problem assumes the extension.
- ABLM 2025 ``Analytically weak and mild solutions...'' proves weak
  regularized and mild regularized solution notions coincide in the
  known existence range; may be useful for local-time-to-mild
  identification, though it still does not by itself remove the
  exceptional-set issue.



\section*{Additional reduction lemmas and strategy notes}

Strong-Feller/resolvent-Feller reduction.  Since $\pi$ is invariant and
has full support, if $A$ is Borel and $\pi(A)=0$, then
\[
  \int P_t{\bf1}_A\,d\pi=\pi(A)=0,\qquad
  \int R_\lambda{\bf1}_A\,d\pi=\lambda^{-1}\pi(A)=0.
\]
Thus either of the following immediately upgrades $\pi$-a.e. null-set
avoidance to all starting points:
\[
  P_t:B_b(E)\to C_b(E)\quad \hbox{for every }t>0,
\]
or
\[
  R_\lambda:B_b(E)\to C_b(E)\quad \hbox{for some }\lambda>0.
\]
Indeed a nonnegative continuous function with zero integral against a
full-support probability is identically zero.  This proves
$P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\pi$ for all $x,t>0$ in the strong-Feller case, and
$R_\lambda(x,\cdot)\ll\pi$ in the resolvent-Feller case.

Entrance reduction.  Let $N$ be a BZ exceptional set such that the ABLM
and BZ resolvents are identified for $y\notin N$.  If
\[
  P_s^P(x,N)=0,\qquad x\in E,\ s>0,
\]
then all-starting-point resolvent absolute continuity follows.  For
$\pi(A)=0$,
\[
 R_\lambda(x,A)
 =\int_0^\varepsilon e^{-\lambda t}P_t(x,A)\,dt
  +e^{-\lambda\varepsilon}P_\varepsilon R_\lambda(\cdot,A)(x).
\]
The first term is at most $\varepsilon$ and the second term is at most
$\lambda^{-1}P_\varepsilon(x,N)$, hence zero; let
$\varepsilon\downarrow0$.

ABLM method note.  The ABLM 2024 paper explicitly says that its proof
uses neither Girsanov nor Zvonkin transformations; therefore one should
not rely on an unstated global Zvonkin homeomorphism to transfer strong
Feller or transition equivalence from an OU process.  Any strong-Feller
argument would have to be proved separately, probably using Malliavin,
capacity, or stochastic-sewing/Krylov estimates.

OU comparison.  For the linear Dirichlet stochastic heat equation,
\[
  Z_t=S_t x+\int_0^t S_{t-s}\,dW_s,
\]
the law at time $t>0$ is equivalent to $\gamma$.  In the sine basis with
Dirichlet eigenvalues $\lambda_k=(k\pi)^2$, the covariance eigenvalues are
$q_k(t)=(1-e^{-\lambda_k t})/\lambda_k$ up to the convention-dependent
factor, while $\gamma$ has eigenvalues proportional to $\lambda_k^{-1}$.
The covariance ratio differs from $1$ by a summable sequence and the mean
$S_t x$ is in the Cameron--Martin space $H^1_0$, so Feldman--Hajek gives
equivalence.  Thus it is enough, in principle, to control the singular
drift contribution relative to this Gaussian law.

Uniform-density route.  A sufficient estimate for the regularized
semigroups is: for every $t>0$ and $x\in E$, there exist $p>1$ and
$C_{t,x}<\infty$ such that
\[
  \sup_n \left\|
    \frac{dP_t^n(x,\cdot)}{d\pi_n}
  \right\|_{L^p(\pi_n)}\le C_{t,x}.
\]
Together with $\pi_n\to\pi$ in total variation and weak convergence
$P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)$, this implies
$P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\pi$.  A capacity version
\[
  P_t^n(x,O)\le C_{t,x}\operatorname{Cap}_n(O)^\alpha
\]
uniformly in $n$ would also imply entrance from BZ exceptional sets,
because the forms have densities bounded above and below relative to
Brownian bridge and hence comparable capacities.

Naive Girsanov issue.  For fixed $n$, the regularized drift
$G_{1/n}(u)$ is bounded and $H$-valued, so pathwise Girsanov from the
linear equation gives absolute continuity.  The usual Novikov/entropy
term contains
\[
  \int_0^t\|G_{1/n}(u_s)\|_{L^2(0,1)}^2\,ds,
\]
which may blow as $n\to\infty$ because $G_{1/n}^2$ approximates a
multiple of $\sqrt n\,\delta_0$.  Any successful limiting argument must
use cancellation or estimates depending on the bounded potential
$0\le B_n\le1$, not on $\|G_{1/n}\|_\infty$ or $\|G_{1/n}\|_2$.

Dirichlet-form comparison idea.  The regularized and limiting invariant
densities are uniformly bounded above and below relative to Brownian
bridge.  Hence the forms
\[
  \mathcal E_n(F,F)=\frac12\int\|DF\|_{L^2}^2\,d\pi_n,\qquad
  \mathcal E_0(F,F)=\frac12\int\|DF\|_{L^2}^2\,d\gamma
\]
have comparable $1$-energies and capacities.  This gives spectral-gap
and compact-resolvent information uniformly, but by itself only produces
kernels and absolute continuity in the $L^2(\pi)$ or quasi-everywhere
sense.  The missing upgrade is a strict, all-starting-points statement.



\section*{Additional reductions for the exceptional-set gap}

Eventual equicontinuity/e-property route.  Suppose $P_t$ is already known
to be reversible with invariant $\pi$, and $P_t f\to\pi(f)$ in
$L^2(\pi)$ for every bounded Lipschitz $f$.  If for every $x\in E$ and
$\varepsilon>0$ there are a neighbourhood $U$ of $x$ and $t_0$ such that
\[
  |P_t f(y)-P_t f(x)|\le \varepsilon,\qquad y\in U,\quad t\ge t_0,
\]
then $P_t f(x)\to\pi(f)$ for every $x$.  The proof avoids compactness:
since $\pi$ has full support, $\pi(U)>0$, and
\[
 |P_t f(x)-\pi(f)|
 \le \varepsilon+
 \left(\pi(U)^{-1}\int_U |P_t f(y)-\pi(f)|^2\,\pi(dy)\right)^{1/2}.
\]
The second term goes to $0$.  Then any invariant law $\eta$ satisfies
$\eta(f)=\int P_t f\,d\eta\to\pi(f)$; bounded Lipschitz functions
separate probability measures on $E$.  This route would prove uniqueness
without all-starting-point absolute continuity.  The analytic missing
input is the eventual equicontinuity estimate.  Ordinary finite-time
Feller continuity is not enough, because the modulus may deteriorate as
$t\to\infty$.

Possible use of ABLM stability estimates.  In the ABLM uniqueness proof
for two solutions driven by the same noise and with the same initial
condition, the local estimate has the form
\[
\|z_t-P_{t-s}z_s\|\lesssim
D_{s,t}(t-s)^{1/2+\delta}+D_{s,t}|\log D_{s,t}|(t-s),
\]
where $D_{s,t}=\sup_{r\in[s,t]}\|z_r\|$.  For different initial data the
same local identity should still hold for the nonlinear drift part,
while $P_tz_0$ remains as an inhomogeneous term.  This suggests finite
time continuous dependence (hence weak Feller), perhaps by an Osgood
argument.  It does not immediately imply the eventual equicontinuity
needed above; one would need either a uniform-in-time modulus or an
asymptotic-strong-Feller estimate in which the heat semigroup
dissipation beats the logarithmic loss.

Capacity-smooth invariant measures route.  Let $N$ be the BZ exceptional
set on whose complement the resolvent kernel represents the actual
resolvent and is absolutely continuous with respect to $\pi$.  It is
enough to show that every invariant probability $\eta$ is smooth for the
BZ Dirichlet form, in particular $\eta(N)=0$.  A sufficient finite-energy
inequality is
\[
  \left|\int \varphi\,d\eta\right|
  \le C_\eta \mathcal E_1(\varphi,\varphi)^{1/2}
\]
for bounded nonnegative quasi-continuous $\varphi\in D(\mathcal E)$.
Indeed, applying this to equilibrium potentials gives
\[
  \eta(O)\le C_\eta\,\operatorname{Cap}(O)^{1/2}
\]
for open $O$, hence $\eta$ charges no zero-capacity set.  Once
$\eta(N)=0$, for any $\pi$-null set $A$,
\[
  \eta(A)=\lambda\int_{N^c}R_\lambda(y,A)\,\eta(dy)=0,
\]
so $\eta\ll\pi$ and the $L^2(\pi)$ spectral gap forces $\eta=\pi$.
A possible way to prove the finite-energy inequality would be a
stationary Krylov/capacity estimate for the regularized processes with
constants depending on the bounded primitives $B_n$ rather than on
$\|G_{1/n}\|_\infty$.

Local-time form of the drift.  Bounebache--Zambotti write the SPDE with
a local-time-in-space term
\[
 -\frac12\int_\mathbb R f(da)\,\partial_\theta \ell^a_{t,\theta}.
\]
For $f=-2H_+$, $df=-2\delta_0$, so this term is
$\partial_\theta\ell^0_{t,\theta}$, which is formally
$\delta_0(u(t,\theta))$ by the occupation density formula in the spatial
variable.  Thus their Dirichlet form matches the Gibbs measure
$\pi\propto\exp(2\int_0^1{\bf1}_{\{v>0\}})\,d\gamma$, but their Markov
solution is constructed only outside a properly exceptional set.

Finite-dimensional projection/entropy route.  For an invariant law
$\eta$, finite-dimensional projections onto the first $m$ Dirichlet
modes should have Lebesgue densities because the projected dynamics has
a nondegenerate Brownian martingale part.  This by itself is insufficient
for $\eta\ll\pi$ in infinite dimensions.  A useful target would be
uniform relative entropy
\[
  \sup_m H((\Pi_m)_\#\eta\mid(\Pi_m)_\#\pi)<\infty,
\]
which would imply $\eta\ll\pi$ by projective convergence of entropy.
The difficulty is to derive such a bound from the stationary martingale
problem with the distributional drift; the drift contribution is a
surface/local-time measure rather than an $L^2$ vector field.

Roughness decomposition idea.  At fixed $t>0$, the linear stochastic
convolution has a Gaussian law equivalent to Brownian bridge.  If the
drift convolution were an independent Cameron--Martin shift, absolute
continuity would follow immediately.  In reality it is adapted and
depends on the same noise.  A possible approach is to split the driving
noise into independent high-frequency or late-time pieces and show that
the singular drift contribution cannot cancel the nondegenerate Gaussian
roughness.  This would amount to a Malliavin or conditional-density
argument for the singular/local-time SPDE.



\section*{Round 4 refined reductions and obstacles}

The finite-energy/capacity shortcut must be formulated carefully.  An
inequality involving quasi-continuous versions
\[
  \left|\int \tilde\varphi\,d\eta\right|
  \le C_\eta \mathcal E_1(\varphi,\varphi)^{1/2}
\]
is not well-defined before knowing that $\eta$ does not charge exceptional
sets, because $\tilde\varphi$ is only defined up to capacity-zero sets.
A safer non-circular criterion is a capacity--Krylov estimate: for every
compact $K\subset E$, $0<\varepsilon<T$, and open $O\subset E$,
\[
 \sup_{x\in K}\mathbf E_x\int_\varepsilon^T{\bf1}_O(u_s)\,ds
 \le C_{K,\varepsilon,T}\operatorname{Cap}(O)^\alpha .
\]
Then any invariant $\eta$ avoids zero-capacity sets: choose compact $K$
with $\eta(K^c)<\delta$ and use stationarity to bound
\[
 (T-\varepsilon)\eta(O)
 \le C_{K,\varepsilon,T}\operatorname{Cap}(O)^\alpha
      +(T-\varepsilon)\delta .
\]
For $\operatorname{Cap}(N)=0$, outer regularity gives open
$O\supset N$ with the first term arbitrarily small for this fixed $K$,
then $\delta\downarrow0$.  This avoids the circular equilibrium-potential
argument.

Absolute continuity of BZ resolvent q.e. plus finite-time weak Feller is
not enough.  If $x_m\to x$ and $P_t(x_m,\cdot)\ll\pi$, weak convergence
of $P_t(x_m,\cdot)$ to $P_t(x,\cdot)$ preserves zero mass only for open
$\pi$-null sets, which are essentially useless since $\pi$ has full
support.  Weak limits of absolutely continuous measures may be singular.

For fixed-time absolute continuity, the mild decomposition
\[
  u_t=S_tu_0+Z_t+\int_0^t S_{t-s}\,\delta_0(u_s)\,ds
\]
suggests that the drift convolution is smoother than the Brownian-bridge
rough part $Z_t$.  However a random Cameron--Martin shift depending on
the Gaussian variable need not preserve absolute continuity: in an
abstract Wiener space, the map that removes one Gaussian coordinate is
of the form $w\mapsto w+F(w)$ with $F(w)$ Cameron--Martin-valued, yet its
image is supported on a hyperplane.  Thus one needs adaptedness,
invertibility, Malliavin nondegeneracy, or a time-splitting argument; mere
pathwise Cameron--Martin regularity of the drift convolution is
insufficient.

Stationary Fokker--Planck route.  Classical Bogachev--Roeckner type
regularity theorems for invariant measures of infinite-dimensional
diffusions can imply absolute continuity with respect to a Gaussian
reference measure when the perturbing drift is Cameron--Martin/noise-Hilbert
valued and square integrable with respect to the invariant measure.  The
present drift $\delta_0(u(\cdot))$ is a spatial local-time measure, not an
$L^2(0,1)$ vector field; the natural Gibbs density is bounded but need
not have finite Fisher information for the $L^2$-gradient Dirichlet form.
Thus those theorems do not apply directly without a new argument adapted
to bounded-variation potentials.

A potentially useful invariant-measure-only strategy is to start the
regularized equation from an arbitrary invariant $\eta$ of the limiting
semigroup.  Then $\eta P_t^n\Rightarrow\eta$ for each fixed $t$ by (A).
If one had a uniform-in-$n$ entropy, $L^p$-density, or capacity estimate
for $\eta P_t^n$ relative to $\pi_n$ on compact subsets of initial data,
then $\eta\ll\pi$ would follow.  This is equivalent in strength to the
capacity--Krylov estimate above.  Naive Girsanov or Bismut constants for
$G_{1/n}$ blow up; any successful estimate must use the bounded primitive
$0\le B_n\le1$ rather than $\|G_{1/n}\|_\infty$.

\end{document}


```

summary.text:
**Reviewing nonlinear contributions**

I'm looking at how the split, especially in the nonlinear contributions from the last interval, is smoother and relatively small. It seems like the older part is independent of late noise. Although there's some dependence in the last nonlinear interval, it might be that the last term is smoother and negligible overall, judging by conditional densities. I should check references, particularly "MattinglyRomitoSu2022BurgersGaussian," since it likely discusses proving law equivalence to Gaussian using "shifted measures and Besov regularity." There are interesting connections with stochastic Burgers equations too!

**Exploring Gaussian law and shifts**

I'm considering whether I should cite Mattingly-Romito-Su's work on late-noise decomposition, especially regarding the smallness and Hölder regularity of the nonlinear remainder. They rely on a Besov support theorem, so I want to be accurate. I'm thinking through this abstract problem: if X = G + Y(G), with G being Gaussian and Y(G) smoother, does it imply that the law of X always follows the law of G? 

I realized that a counterexample could involve a coordinate collapse in infinite Gaussian spaces. It's a complex idea, showing how shifts can affect regularity by modifying finite coordinates, but if it's dependent on the same coordinate, it may collapse.

**Assessing coordinate shifts**

I'm thinking about how lower and upper triangular conditions might help if the coordinate shift depends only on previous coordinates. In this case, the conditional law of that coordinate would shift with those prior ones. However, the natural PDE nonlinearity could imply that high modes relate more to high noise, potentially complicating the triangular condition. 

I wonder if I can split the terminal Gaussian into low and high modes, conditioning on the high part. The drift coefficient, influenced by preceding noise, might not guarantee triangularity. It seems the high frequencies of terminal output are mainly generated by very late noise, leaving little time for the drift coefficient to effectively influence them.

**Exploring triangular high-mode proof**

I’m considering the triangular high-mode proof, where I could choose blocks of modes k and reserve a last interval of length c/λ_k to randomize mode k after most of the drift occurs. However, I wonder if drift during that tiny interval can also depend on the same noise, affecting mode k too. 

The drift coefficient being bounded might contribute to mode k over δ=1/λ_k, which could be on the order of O(1/λ_k). If I'm quantifying the noise standard deviation for mode k, I should ensure it remains manageable while considering the drift contributions associated with each mode's coefficient. I'll need to dig deeper into that!

**Analyzing drift in SDEs**

I'm considering the measures in a one-dimensional SDE, specifically how if measure a=0, it's negligible, but if a=1, the drift's comparable. I'm wondering if this adapted drift contributes to density as long as the energy remains finite over δ. 

For the drift coefficient b_k, its size might relate to k^a, leading to energy estimates that need to be manageable across modes. To prove infinite product equivalence, I need to focus on summing Hellinger distances. I think high-mode estimates are necessary! I’m contemplating a potential architecture with control and triangular time blocks for expanded sine modes.

**Evaluating block drift and energy**

I'm exploring how the block drift, when it has finite L2 energy with uniform exponential moments, allows Girsanov to give conditional AC for block coordinates. However, multiplying over blocks demands summable Hellinger or entropy measures, and I think heat damping could minimize the drift effect in its reserved window to something like O(N^{-η}). 

I need to estimate δ0 for block drift energy. It seems the expected block L2 norm could depend on the number of modes, and I wonder about the implications of μ being a finite measure. There might be a concern if the local time measure is a derivative!

**Analyzing BZ term and convergence**

I’m thinking about the BZ term being possibly a derivative, yet in this problem, δ0(u) lacks a spatial derivative, suggesting its measure order is likely a=0, which is promising. 

With this architecture, I’m considering fixed-time AC and the need to stabilize the regularized limit using local time/occupation bounds for the projected δ. I need to estimate E ∫_{I_N} ||Π_N G_n(u_s)||^2 ds ≤ C N |I_N|, while questioning if this holds independent of n. The approximation of G_n could blow up, but projecting beforehand might help. It all leads to the need for spatial local time estimates of u_s for convergence. It feels complex but doable!

**Considering singular drift and estimates**

I'm reflecting on the Krylov-Röckner work regarding singular drift and thinking that ABLM 2024 might prove well-posedness through "regularization by noise," which is an alternative to Girsanov. They might have useful estimates for regularized drift integrals that I can use to bound the dyadic block energy.

Alternatively, I consider fixed-time AC using Malliavin calculus. I wonder if uniform negative moments are necessary, since the drift derivative G'_n could be unbounded. The pathwise uniqueness might not be enough, and I’m also thinking about whether strong Feller conditions for singular SPDE by Hairer-Mattingly apply here.

**Finalizing the proof architecture**

I'm thinking that while the late-noise approach seems plausible in principle, it can't rely on random CM shifts. The only theorems I trust are adapted Girsanov or Ramer/Üstünel, both needing finite energy or a Malliavin derivative. For δ0, the full path energy diverges, complicating things. So, a naive one-window splitting won't work; I need a multiscale late-noise and block triangular argument.

To structure my proof, I plan to use regularized equations and sine dyadic projections. I’ll condition on past information and independent increments in disjoint terminal windows, which will then generate fresh Gaussian variables. Finally, I want to show that the solution for each block is a deterministic function of the conditioned variables, previous increments, and an adapted drift term.

**Refining the conditional law**

The conditional law of the block under drift is absolutely continuous with respect to η_j, utilizing finite-dimensional Girsanov. I need to bound the Hellinger/entropy cost by estimating E∫_{I_j} ||P_j δ0(u_s)||^2 ds. 

Next, I want to show that the summability ∑_j cost_j is finite and uniform with respect to regularization or the conditioned past. I'll use the local-time spatial regularity since δ0(u_s(·)) is a finite measure. The dyadic projection squared norm is roughly 2^j ||μ_s||_{TV}^2 over a length of 2^{-2j}, leading to a cost of about 2^{-j}.

Older drift and past terms stay manageable due to heat smoothing. Using infinite product/Kakutani or martingale density convergence, I can derive conditional absolute continuity for the full terminal law with respect to the Brownian bridge. I have to keep track of key estimates, especially the uniform local-time bound for μ_s = δ0(u_s) dθ. If the actual term is ∂θℓ^0, which has a worse spatial derivative, then the cost might not be summable, jeopardizing the whole architecture.

**Examining late noise and drift**

It seems the obstruction is quite sharp: the late noise is on the same scale as the drift in high modes, which means there's no Gaussian equivalence from this method. I need to reconcile what BZ says about the term ∂θ ℓ. For f = -2H+, there’s the singular term ∂θℓ^0. 

Now, is δ0(u) actually equal to ∂θ local time? If X(θ) behaves like a semimartingale (think Brownian bridge), the occupation density connects through integration with local time. 

So, δ0(X_θ) dθ corresponds to d_θ ℓ^0_θ, which is a measure of the local time in θ. Pairing this gives us an interesting connection: ∫ h(θ) δ0(X_θ)dθ equals ∫ h dℓ^0_θ. I wonder, does this mean ∂θℓ as a distribution? The BZ term indicates a connection, where integration with f being the measure δ0 results in ∂θ ℓ^0, and pairing it with h helps form the equation. Hence, ∂θ ℓ^0 can indeed be seen as a measure!

**Exploring Fourier coefficients and local time**

The Fourier coefficients with e_k reveal that <∂θ ℓ, e_k> equals -∫ ℓ e'_k if ℓ is continuous. But as a measure dℓ, when paired with e_k, I get ∫ e_k dℓ. There seems to be no growth in crude total variation. If I think about the distribution derivative of continuous local time, then it appears that total variation equals the final local time? Since ℓ is increasing in θ, this gives us a finite positive measure.

Therefore, μ is a finite measure with a=0, which is encouraging! I need to mention that the total mass of local time at 0 could be a random unbounded variable. For general x, especially conditioned on periods like [t-ε,t], it's crucial to note that while the moments may be finite, they are not guaranteed to be uniform conditionally. 

When considering AC (absolutely continuous) properties, density can actually be random with finite expectation. The conditional Girsanov needs ∫ to be finite almost surely (not necessarily exponential). In a finite-dimensional Brownian setting with adapted drift that maintains finite energy, the law may be AC if the stochastic exponential martingale is in play. Novikov's condition is sufficient, and I can localize this situation, which is promising!

**Assessing block summability and Girsanov's theorem**

For a finite time and block, it seems we might have finite energy. If I consider weak Girsanov with linear growth, it could work. To ensure block product summability, I need the entropy to be finite in expectation, and local time moments appear sufficient. 

I'll move from conditional AC of dyadic blocks to using the Hellinger process. If the conditional block densities ρ_j relate to independent Gaussian blocks and the sum is finite, then product density should converge. I need to investigate if I can formulate the relative entropy over the filtration of modes while ensuring that the total density remains finite. If the relative entropy is bounded uniformly, this leads to a weak limit indicating a nice result!

**Exploring entropy control and drift projection**

I'm considering a potential obstacle: Can the entropy relative to a terminal Gaussian be managed by path Girsanov energy concerning the projected drift across all modes? The full drift energy might be infinite, but for each finite J, it looks like path Girsanov could still work for the projected equation. 

I need to pay attention to the drift projected to the first N energy, which scales with N but remains finite. I also have to think about a uniform N bound and how I can utilize reserved windows to work with terminal fresh noise instead of full intervals, as older noise is smoothed out. I might condition and then apply block Girsanov on smaller windows, possibly even splitting at t-ε to handle older parts more effectively, smoothing them into a nice manageable state!

**Considering time-slicing and high-mode drift**

I’m weighing the idea of using time-slicing per mode to focus on high-mode drift during late windows, treating the drift before that as a CM shift. This seems essential! I could describe this as not just a single late interval but rather a parabolic multiscale late-noise splitting. 

The challenge is that the drift over the last ε projected onto block j has energy ε 2^j, leading to a divergence when summed. If I assume a deterministic CM shift, it wouldn't be a problem, but the dependence complicates things. I might need quasi-invariance or stick with a multi-scale approach to avoid derivatives, which is a key consideration! If the drift over the last ε remains manageable, Ramer might still work as long as the Malliavin derivative stays in a nice trace class, right?

**Analyzing multi-scale drift and noise**

I'm considering that the multi-scale process only uses adaptedness and energy for the last small windows, treating older drift as (conditionally) deterministic regarding fresh block noise. The drift from times before I_j might depend on earlier noise windows for other blocks but not on reserved I_j. If I place I_j near the terminal, then drift before I_j remains conditionally deterministic. 

However, its block shift is CM and independent of fresh η_j. I also need to discuss that although windows should be nested near t, making them disjoint is tough when infinitely many accumulate. When defining I_j, I might center it around the interval (t-c2^{-2j}, t-c2^{-2(j+1)}) if the window length is 2^{-2j}. If the frequencies are around 2^j, can the heat kernel from that window reach the final covariance? I’m trying to clarify this!

**Considering noise contributions and drift**

I'm thinking about how to choose annuli in backward time for each j, ensuring they're disjoint and accumulating at time t. The chosen intervals, r∈[a 2^{-2j}, b 2^{-2j}], need to avoid including points after t. The noise in this annulus contributes a fixed fraction of full variance, approximately 1/k². Drift after the annulus, which is closer to t, also depends on this noise, so it cannot be treated as entirely independent.

Even though the length after the annulus remains small, it’s essential to consider it within the Girsanov framework. I wonder about conditioning sequentially from low to high; the nested future of higher windows does include lower ones, despite the technical challenges presented by parabolic annuli.

**Summarizing measure transformations and obstacles**

I'm thinking about not going too deep into details. The known theorem, "Üstünel–Zakai, Transformation of Measure on Wiener Space," relates to adapted shifts, while Ramer handles anticipative cases. The "Föllmer drift" concept ties to finite entropy, and there's no existing theorem for distributional Nemytskii δ0 in fixed-time AC for Markov semilinear SPDE. I might mention Da Prato-Zabczyk/Girsanov for L2 drifts. 

There could be a sharp obstruction if drift's dyadic projection energy over parabolic windows isn't summable. For late noise proofs, if the spatial local time measure has rough Fourier tails, that might complicate things. I need to clarify that too.

**Planning the final structure**

I'm outlining my final structure. First, I wouldn't recommend using a one-shot random CM shift or standard Malliavin, as pathwise adapted Girsanov fails due to δ0 not being in L2, making the approximating energy diverge. The Ramer/Malliavin derivative for approximants can't handle this, so no known black-box theorem exists.

However, I think a late-noise argument could be reasonable if I work with a parabolic multiscale triangular split. I'll need to state a key estimate, and I might even include the equation. For dyadic block Π_j with terminal window length τ_j2^{-2j}, I’ll show how to ensure the sum of expectations remains finite.

**Defining the central lemma**

Here’s the central lemma. Using finite-dimensional adapted Girsanov on each block or window allows me to derive conditional densities. The entropy chain rule and product convergence help establish the terminal law's AC regarding Gaussian Zε. The older terms become conditional CM shifts with summable CM norms due to heat smoothing after πγ is completed.

I need to consider potential failures in my central estimate. If, for instance, the drift has an extra spatial derivative leading to block norms scaling as 2^{3j}, the cost wouldn't be summable, and this structure could break down. In that case, the dependence might collapse, and regularity won't assist. 

I’ve seen similar frameworks in the literature, like the Mattingly-Romito-Su Gaussian structure with its parabolic high/late split, while tools like Üstünel-Zakai and Ramer are abstract yet not directly applicable. The BZ and FOT only provide a quasi-everywhere (q.e.) result, lacking an all-start black-box for distributional drift.

I also want to incorporate "Föllmer entropy" or "Kakutani" as a product. I'll keep my write-up below 600 words, aiming for around 550, while being precise about Girsanov density and entropy. I’ll employ regularized equations to sidestep defining δ, letting b^n=G_{1/n}(u^n).

**Analyzing projected drift and entropy**

For block j, I'm focusing on the short window I_j and how projected finite-dimensional drift Π_j impacts b^n. The Girsanov entropy is bounded above by 1/2 E∫ ||Π_j b^n||². I need to ensure that there's uniformity across n and that summability is maintained. 

However, I should be cautious because the norm of the b^n projection might blow up as n increases. My central local time estimate involves keeping track of the sup_n E∫_{I_j}||Π_j G_{1/n}(u_s^n)||² ds, which I hope is ≤ C 2^{-j}. I might need to consider positive time and initial compactness.

If every starting point x is considered, the constants will depend on x and t. This feels like a concrete target. The older nonlinear contributions from before I_j are measurable with respect to conditioning and in the CM framework. They remain deterministic or random shifts that are independent of fresh Gaussian conditions, allowing for ordinary Cameron-Martin conditions in the block sum. 

If the shift depends on earlier windows, I still think I can treat it as a conditional CM shift. I want to clarify that having "fixed-time AC is sufficient" because the resolvent can be obtained through integration.

It appears there's a potential challenge in proving the full law AC from the entropy of block conditionals related to the Gaussian bridge and low modes. I wonder if I can leverage the finite-dimensional Girsanov/Malliavin approach for just a few low modes and apply the path-adapted Girsanov for projected drift with finite energy.

**Clarifying projection and noise structure**

I'm looking at the projection of δ0's finite-dimensional coefficients, with a focus on local time totals and finite energy over a specific interval. It seems like approximants may need to be uniform. Alternatively, additive noise with distributional drift could have a density based on a one-block argument. I need to ensure there's no summability issue with low modes by localizing.

I'll mention that the Malliavin covariance approach might be less effective due to G'_n. It’s important to finalize the formulation elegantly.  

When considering all starting points at fixed time t, it’s essential that the initial x is deterministically arbitrary. I should probably allow for some waiting time before local time moments, especially if x is pathological. If the additive noise regularizes immediately in θ, I might run into issues near s=0 if x has a large zero set.

For a late split with ε < t, I plan to condition at t - ε. I should consider the state at the start as random but continuous over the interval length ε. I also want local time moments on this late interval conditioned on an arbitrary y.

If y is continuous, then the noise behaves in a Brownian-like manner. I think constants might depend on y, and after conditioning, I should integrate. Ultimately, for all starting points, I need to ensure almost sure finiteness relative to y.

**Structuring the final draft**

I’m considering that uniformity on compact sets could be a good approach for invariance, and I think that might work well. content.text:
I would not try to justify this as a one-shot “random Cameron–Martin shift”. The right adapted theorem would be path-space Girsanov/Üstünel–Zakai for adapted shifts, but it requires finite \(L^2([t-\varepsilon,t];L^2)\)-energy of the drift. For the regularisations \(G_{1/n}(u)\) this energy blows like the square of an approximate delta, so that route is not stable. A Ramer/Malliavin map theorem also looks unattractive: the derivative contains \(G'_{1/n}(u)\), and I would expect determinant/negative-moment bounds to blow up.

The late-noise idea may still be viable, but I think it has to be **parabolic multiscale/triangular**, not a single split. A concrete architecture:

1. Work first with the regularised equations and expand in Dirichlet sine blocks \(\Pi_j\), \(|k|\sim 2^j\).

2. For block \(j\), reserve only a terminal time window of parabolic length
   \[
   \tau_j\sim 2^{-2j}.
   \]
   The noise from this window contributes a Gaussian component to \(\Pi_j u_t\) with covariance comparable to the Brownian-bridge covariance on that block.

3. Condition on the past and on the other noise pieces. On this finite-dimensional block/window, use ordinary adapted Girsanov. The entropy/Hellinger cost is controlled by
   \[
   \int_{t-\tau_j}^t \|\Pi_j G_{1/n}(u_s^n)\|_{L^2}^2\,ds .
   \]

4. Prove the key summable estimate, uniformly in \(n\),
   \[
   \sum_j \mathbb E\int_{t-c2^{-2j}}^t
        \|\Pi_j G_{1/n}(u_s^n)\|_{L^2}^2\,ds <\infty,
   \]
   or a conditional/localised version. Heuristically this is exactly where the local-time structure helps: if \(\delta_0(u_s(\theta))\,d\theta\) is the spatial local-time measure \(d_\theta \ell^0_s\), then
   \[
   \|\Pi_j d\ell^0_s\|_{L^2}^2 \lesssim 2^j \|\ell^0_s\|_{\mathrm{TV}}^2,
   \]
   and multiplying by \(\tau_j\sim2^{-2j}\) gives \(O(2^{-j})\), summable.

5. Use the entropy chain rule/Kakutani product criterion to pass from blockwise conditional absolute continuity to full terminal absolute continuity relative to the Gaussian bridge law. Older drift contributions are then conditionally Cameron–Martin with summable block norm because of heat smoothing.

This is close in spirit to the “Gaussian structure” arguments of Mattingly–Romito–Su for singular Burgers: fresh parabolic-scale noise beats the nonlinear remainder block by block. The central hard lemma is the uniform dyadic local-time estimate above. If the BZ drift were effectively one spatial derivative rougher, giving \(\|\Pi_j b\|^2\sim 2^{3j}\), the scheme would fail sharply. But for the measure \(d_\theta\ell^0\) scaling, it looks plausible.

I do not know a black-box theorem giving all-starting fixed-time AC for this distributional Nemytskii drift. BZ/FOT give quasi-everywhere resolvent AC; standard SPDE strong-Feller/Malliavin theorems do not seem to apply directly. So I would present this as a concrete possible proof route, with the dyadic projection/local-time energy estimate as the make-or-break input.
